title: Migration Dynamics and Quality of Life Index
text:
2.1 Data Source
The datasets used in the project are:
1.Population size of the provinces
2.Migration received by provinces according to the reason for migration
3.Migration rates by provinces according to the reason for migration
4.[Net migration rate for provinces according to reasons for migration] Calculated as “Migration Outgoing - Migration Incoming”
3.Life Index Rankings and Index Values by Province

title: Migration Dynamics and Quality of Life Index
text:
2.2 General Information About Data
Our dataset tracks migration metrics across all 81 provinces of Türkiye from 2018 to 2024. It combines raw inbound/outbound migration figures with detailed columns explaining 12 different reasons for migration, cross-references them with the Turkish Statistical Institute’s (TÜİK) comprehensive Life Index scores, and shows the relationships between them.

title: Migration Dynamics and Quality of Life Index
text:
2.3 Reason of Choice
We chose this data because migration cannot be explained solely by economic factors. By combining net migration metrics with the multidimensional Life Index (which assesses health, education, security, and environment), we can reveal the relationship between internal migration in Türkiye and life indices.

title: Migration Dynamics and Quality of Life Index
text:
2.4 Preprocessing
The net migration values are calculated using migration data received and given by provinces in different Excel spreadsheets and the char formatted values containing spaces in the data were updated to numeric values.


Code
#install.packages("ggplot2") 
#install.packages("dplyr")   
#install.packages("openxlsx")
#install.packages("ggrepel")
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(readxl) 
library(writexl) 
library(stringr)
library(forcats)
library(tidyr)
library(openxlsx)
library(ggplot2)
library(readxl)
library(dplyr)
library(ggrepel)



2.4.1. Calculating net migration values and creating a separate Excel file and checking and correcting numerical values.


Code
VerilenGoc <- read_excel("C:/EMU660_Project/emu660-spring2026-Selindznn/VerilenGoc.xlsx", col_names = TRUE)
AlinanGoc  <- read_excel("C:/EMU660_Project/emu660-spring2026-Selindznn/AlinanGoc.xlsx", col_names = TRUE)
toplam_sutun <- ncol(VerilenGoc)
fark_df <- VerilenGoc 
for (i in 3:toplam_sutun) {
  v_clean <- as.character(VerilenGoc[[i]])
  v_clean <- str_replace_all(v_clean, "\\s", "") 
  VerilenGoc[[i]] <- as.numeric(v_clean)        
  a_clean <- as.character(AlinanGoc[[i]])
  a_clean <- str_replace_all(a_clean, "\\s", "")    
  AlinanGoc[[i]] <- as.numeric(a_clean)
}
fark_df[, 3:toplam_sutun] <- VerilenGoc[, 3:toplam_sutun] - AlinanGoc[, 3:toplam_sutun]
for (i in 3:toplam_sutun) {
  fark_df[[i]] <- as.numeric(fark_df[[i]])
}
write_xlsx(fark_df, "C:/EMU660_Project/emu660-spring2026-Selindznn/NetGoc.xlsx")
print(VerilenGoc[, 3:toplam_sutun])

2.4.2. Calculating net migration rate using population data of provinces and creating an Excel file.


Code
kaynak_yolu <- "C:/EMU660_Project/emu660-spring2026-Selindznn/NetGoc.xlsx"
hedef_yolu  <- "C:/EMU660_Project/emu660-spring2026-Selindznn/IlNufuslarii.xlsx"

df_kaynak <- read_excel(kaynak_yolu, col_names = TRUE)

NetGocVerisi <- df_kaynak[1:82, 3, drop = TRUE] 
wb <- loadWorkbook(hedef_yolu)
writeData(wb, sheet = 1, x = "Net Göç", startCol = 3, startRow = 1, colNames = FALSE)
writeData(wb, sheet = 1, x = NetGocVerisi, startCol = 3, startRow = 2, colNames = FALSE)

saveWorkbook(wb, hedef_yolu, overwrite = TRUE)

title: Migration Dynamics and Quality of Life Index

3.1.3. Migration Figures by Province in 2024


Code
AlinanGoc  <- read_excel("C:/EMU660_Project/emu660-spring2026-Selindznn/AlinanGoc.xlsx",col_names = TRUE)

colnames(AlinanGoc)[1:3] <- c("Yil", "Il", "Goc_Miktari")
veri_2024 <- subset(AlinanGoc, Yil == 2024 & Il != "Toplam-Total")
veri_2024$Goc_Miktari_Bin <- veri_2024$Goc_Miktari / 1000


ggplot(veri_2024, aes(x = Goc_Miktari_Bin, y = reorder(Il, Goc_Miktari_Bin))) +
  geom_col(aes(fill = Goc_Miktari_Bin > 0)) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("coral", "skyblue"), guide = "none") +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "2024 Yılı İllerin Alınan Göç Miktarları",
    x = "Alınan Göç Miktarı (Bin Kişi)",
    y = "İller"
  )











3.1.4. Net Migration Figures of Provinces in 2024
A graphical representation of the calculation made using the previously calculated data given migration-received migration data.


Code
NetGoc  <- read_excel("C:/EMU660_Project/emu660-spring2026-Selindznn/NetGoc.xlsx",col_names = TRUE)

colnames(NetGoc)[1:3] <- c("Yil", "Il", "Goc_Miktari")
veri_2024 <- subset(NetGoc, Yil == 2024 & Il != "Toplam-Total")
veri_2024$Goc_Miktari_Bin <- veri_2024$Goc_Miktari / 1000


ggplot(veri_2024, aes(x = Goc_Miktari_Bin, y = reorder(Il, Goc_Miktari_Bin))) +
  geom_col(aes(fill = Goc_Miktari_Bin > 0)) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("coral", "skyblue"), guide = "none") +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "2024 Yılı İllerin NEt Göç Miktarları",
    x = "Net Göç Miktarı (Bin Kişi)",
    y = "İller"
  )  











3.1.5. Graph showing the distribution of migration reasons that make up net migration data.
Migration reasons include: change of job, starting/finding a job, change of marital status/family-related reasons, better housing and living conditions, migration related to any member of the household/family, returning to family home/hometown, health/care, buying a house, retirement, other, and unknown.


Code
NetGoc  <- read_excel("C:/EMU660_Project/emu660-spring2026-Selindznn/NetGoc.xlsx",col_names = TRUE)

goc_nedenleri <- c("Tayin/Is Degisikligi", "Ise Baslamak/Is Bulmak", "Egitim", 
                   "Medeni Durum/Ailevi", "Daha Iyi Konut/Yasam", "Haneye Bagimli Goc", 
                   "Memlekete Donus", "Saglik/Bakim", "Ev Alinmasi", 
                   "Emeklilik", "Diger", "Bilinmeyen")

colnames(NetGoc)[1:3] <- c("Yil", "Il", "Toplam_Goc")
colnames(NetGoc)[4:15] <- goc_nedenleri
veri_2024 <- subset(NetGoc, Yil == 2024 & Il != "Toplam-Total")

veri_uzun <- pivot_longer(veri_2024, 
                          cols = 4:15, 
                          names_to = "Goc_Nedeni", 
                          values_to = "Miktar")

veri_uzun$Miktar_Bin <- veri_uzun$Miktar / 1000

goc_grafigi <- ggplot(veri_uzun, aes(x = Miktar_Bin, y = reorder(Il, Toplam_Goc), fill = Goc_Nedeni)) +
  geom_col(position = "stack") +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "2024 Yılı İllerin Göç Nedenlerine Göre Dağılımı",
    subtitle = "Miktarlar bin kişi cinsinden ifade edilmiştir",
    x = "Net Göç Miktarı (Bin Kişi)",
    y = "İller",
    fill = "Göç Nedenleri"
  ) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "bottom",
    legend.text = element_text(size = 8),
    axis.text.y = element_text(size = 7)
  ) +
  guides(fill = guide_legend(ncol = 3))
  
print(goc_grafigi)

title: Migration Dynamics and Quality of Life Index
text:
3.2 Trend Analysis
The graph shows the relational analysis between migration rate data and life indices of the provinces.


Code
yasam_df <- read_excel("C:/EMU660_Project/emu660-spring2026-Selindznn/YasamEndeksi.xlsx", col_names = TRUE)
goc_df <- read_excel("C:/EMU660_Project/emu660-spring2026-Selindznn/Gochizi.xlsx", col_names = TRUE)

colnames(yasam_df)[1:2] <- c("Il", "Endeks")
colnames(goc_df)[1:3]   <- c("Yil", "Il", "Goc")

goc_2024 <- subset(goc_df, Yil == 2024 & Il != "Toplam-Total")

veri_seti <- inner_join(yasam_df, goc_2024, by = "Il")

grafik <- ggplot(veri_seti, aes(x = Endeks, y = Goc, label = Il)) + 
  geom_point(color = "#1f77b4", size = 3, alpha = 0.7) + 
  geom_text_repel(size = 3, max.overlaps = 15) + 
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "red", se = TRUE, formula = y ~ x) + 
  labs(
    title = "İl Yaşam Endeksi ve 2024 Net Göç Hızı İlişkisi",
    x = "Genel Yaşam Endeksi",
    y = "Net Göç Hızı (‰)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

print(grafik)

title: Project X

3.3 Model Fitting
xxxxxx

title: Migration Dynamics and Quality of Life Index
text:
3.4 Results
Our time series analysis (2018–2024) shows that overall migration numbers fluctuate significantly based on national economic shifts and major external events (e.g., pandemics, earthquakes). When we looking at major metropolitan areas like Ankara, we see a distinct pattern of high incoming and high outgoing figures, reflecting its status as a dynamic political and educational hub. Our 2024 province-by-province breakdown confirms that the geographic distribution of migration in Turkey is highly polarized. A small cluster of economically dominant provinces acts as “Net Migration Champions,” while a vast majority of eastern and rural provinces serve as continuous “Migration Sources. The stacked column chart for 2024 reasons for migration provides crucial insights. Rather than simple employment changes (”Tayin/İş Değişikliği”), a massive portion of net migration is driven by:Education (“Eğitim”): Causing heavy youth mobility toward university cities.Better Housing and Living Conditions (“Daha İyi Konut/Yaşam”) Indicating that families are moving reactively to secure better urban standards.Dependent Migration (“Haneye Bağımlı Göç”)Showing that migration is rarely an individual choice but a household-level transition. Our trend analysis using geom_smooth(method = “lm”) reveals a positive correlation between a province’s General Life Index and its Net Migration Rate.

title: Assignment 1
text:
(a)
Video: Veri Bilimi ve Endüstri Mühendisliği Üzerine Sohbetler - Kerem Demirtaş & Erdi Dasdemir
Summary: Data scientist Kerem Demirtaş, a guest on the video, discussed some misconceptions about data science. For example, he mentioned that data science isn’t just statistics or just big data. He emphasized that combining statistics and, when necessary, big data with ML (Material Engineering), an engineering perspective, and industry and field expertise based on the data are all crucial. He also highlighted the importance of the human perspective in interpreting and analyzing data. When applying data science, after data collection and some cleaning stages, EDA (Electronic Data Analysis) is necessary for better understanding and interpretation. After understanding the statistical properties of the data, modeling the data and selecting and testing the model’s success (underfitting/overfitting balance) are necessary. The guest also discussed his doctoral thesis, mentioning how autonomous vehicles optimize traffic by forming convoys (platooning). He explained how autonomous vehicles can communicate with each other to reduce following distances and how this increases road capacity. Additionally, simulations have shown how traffic flow can be improved with a centralized control mechanism in “mixed traffic” scenarios where human drivers and autonomous vehicles coexist.

title: Assignment 1
Assignment 1
text:
(d)
install.packages("dslabs")
library(dslabs)
data(polls_us_election_2016) 
head(polls_us_election_2016)
# Display k rows of the original data set on your web page using this rule:
my_first <- "Selin"
my_birth_year <- 1999
k <- (nchar(my_first) + my_birth_year) %% 15 + 8
print(k)  

if (k %% 2 == 0) { 
  display_k_value <- head(polls_us_election_2016, k)
  message <- paste("k =", k, "adet ilk satırlar")
} else {
  display_k_value <- tail(polls_us_election_2016, k)
  message <- paste("k =", k, "adet son satırlar")
}

cat(message, "\n")
print(display_k_value)
# Calculate and display:
NA_total <- sum(is.na(polls_us_election_2016))
cat("Veri setindeki toplam NA sayısı:", NA_total)

NA_per_column <- colSums(is.na(polls_us_election_2016))
print(NA_per_column)

NA_sort <- sort(NA_per_column, decreasing = TRUE)

top_8_na <- head(NA_sort, 8)

cat("Top 8 column:")
print(top_8_na)
#Replace all NA values in the data set as follows and store the result as a new object
new_polls_data <- polls_us_election_2016

num_fill <- my_birth_year + k
char_fill <- paste0(my_first, "_", k)


for (col in names(new_polls_data))
  if (is.numeric(news_polls_data[[col]])) 
    new_polls_data[[col]][is.na(new_polls_data[[col]])] <- num_fill
    
  else if (is.character(new_polls_data[[col]]))
  new_polls_data[[col]][is.na(new_polls_data[[col]])] <- char_fill

cat("Yeni durumdaki toplam NA sayısı:", sum(is.na(new_polls_data)), "\n")

print(head(new_polls_data, 10))
#Display k rows of the new data frame using the exact same rule as above
if (k %% 2 == 0) { 
  display_newk_value <- head(new_polls_data, k)
  message <- paste("k =", k, "adet ilk satırlar")
} else {
  display_newk_value <- tail(new_polls_data, k)
  message <- paste("k =", k, "adet son satırlar")
}

cat(message, "\n")
print(display_newk_value)
total_na_remaining <- sum(is.na(new_polls_data))
print("Yeni veri çerçevesindeki toplam NA sayısı:", total_na_remaining, "\n")

check_any_na <- anyNA(new_polls_data)
print(check_any_na)

title: Assignment 1
text:
(b)
Q1: In data science, what does the exam example given in the video represent regarding the concept of ‘Overfitting’? A. Failing to finish the exam on time B. Taking the exam without studying for the lectures C. Memorizing past questions and being unable to solve different questions D. Understanding the logic of the questions
Open-ended question: What is the “Abraham Wald and the bomber planes” example mentioned in the video for the “problem definition” stage in data science?
Answer: This example shows that focusing only on the visible data (the planes rotating) can be misleading. Avoiding survival bias, understanding the real issue—“where the downed planes didn’t return”—leads to a more accurate problem definition and finding the right solution (where to add the armor).

title: Assignment 1
text:
(c) mtcars
data(mtcars)
head(mtcars)
# Write a Custom Summary Function
compute_stats <- function(x) {
   if(!is.numeric(x)) {
     print("Sayısal Veri Girilmeli")
   }
   
   named_list <- list(
     mean     = mean(x),
     median   = median(x),
     variance = var(x),
     iqr      = IQR(x),
     min      = min(x),
     max      = max(x)
   )
   
   return(named_list)
 }
compute_stats(mtcars$cyl)
# Applying the Function Using a Loop
df <- mtcars
for (col_name in names(df)) {

 if (is.numeric(df[[col_name]])) {
 stats <- compute_stats(df[[col_name]])
 cat("\n- Statistics for Column:", col_name, "-\n")
 print(stats)
 }
}
# An alternative approach with sapply and apply
just_numeric_cols <- df[sapply(df, is.numeric)]
stats_sapply <- sapply(just_numeric_cols, compute_stats)
print(stats_sapply)
df_matrix <- as.matrix(just_numeric_cols)
stats_apply <- apply(df_matrix, 2, compute_stats)  # apply (x, MARGIN, FUN,,,,simplify= TRUE)
print(stats_apply)

my_first <- "Selin"
my_birth_year <- 1999
# Define k rows
k <- (nchar(my_first) + my_birth_year) %% 15 + 8
print(k)  
# Display k rows of the original data set on your web page using this rule
if (k %% 2 == 0) { 
  display_k_value <- head(polls_us_election_2016, k)
  message <- paste("k =", k, "adet ilk satırlar")
} else {
  display_k_value <- tail(polls_us_election_2016, k)
  message <- paste("k =", k, "adet son satırlar")
}

cat(message, "\n")
print(display_k_value)
# Calculate and display
NA_total <- sum(is.na(polls_us_election_2016))
cat("Veri setindeki toplam NA sayısı:", NA_total)

NA_per_column <- colSums(is.na(polls_us_election_2016))
print(NA_per_column)

NA_sort <- sort(NA_per_column, decreasing = TRUE)

top_8_na <- head(NA_sort, 8)

cat("Top 8 column:")
print(top_8_na)
# Replace all NA values in the data set as follows and store the result as a new object
new_polls_data <- polls_us_election_2016

num_fill <- my_birth_year + k
char_fill <- paste0(my_first, "_", k)


for (col in names(new_polls_data))
  if (is.numeric(news_polls_data[[col]])) 
    new_polls_data[[col]][is.na(new_polls_data[[col]])] <- num_fill
    
  else if (is.character(new_polls_data[[col]]))
  new_polls_data[[col]][is.na(new_polls_data[[col]])] <- char_fill

cat("Yeni durumdaki toplam NA sayısı:", sum(is.na(new_polls_data)), "\n")

print(head(new_polls_data, 10))
# Display k rows of the new data frame using the exact same rule as above
if (k %% 2 == 0) { 
  display_newk_value <- head(new_polls_data, k)
  message <- paste("k =", k, "adet ilk satırlar")
} else {
  display_newk_value <- tail(new_polls_data, k)
  message <- paste("k =", k, "adet son satırlar")
}

cat(message, "\n")
print(display_newk_value)
total_na_remaining <- sum(is.na(new_polls_data)) 
print("Yeni veri çerçevesindeki toplam NA sayısı:", total_na_remaining, "\n")
# Check anyNA (new_polls_data) <- FALSE
check_any_na <- anyNA(new_polls_data)
print(check_any_na)
